narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try based on academic and background factors. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 38 (slightly above the 36.70 average), high family income bracket, full-time student status, and low Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1— and estimated a 41.6% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom tier of your school's grades, signaling weaker performance relative to peers who typically succeed on the bar. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your LSAT score, which had a strong favorable influence by demonstrating solid analytical skills above the norm. Your high family income also pulled in a positive direction, as students from similar backgrounds often show better bar outcomes, perhaps due to additional resources or support. Compared to typical students, your low deciles stand out as a red flag, while your LSAT and income align with or exceed averages that help others pass. Overall, the model weighed your Year 3 performance as the top concern, overriding the strengths in your test score and background. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male applicant—many from similar demographics excel with stronger academic trajectories—but a data-driven call based on patterns in bar success. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as even small improvements there could shift future predictions dramatically. You might also target schools with holistic reviews that value your LSAT edge more heavily. With targeted effort on grades, reapplying next cycle could yield better results. Keep pushing; your profile shows real strengths worth building on.
